ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Singapore economic rice (cai fan) with mixed toppings. FOODON_00002572 (cooked rice dish) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Singapore cai fan. A00LC (rice-based composite dish) is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor for a mixed rice plate with protein and vegetable toppings.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical concept maps to this specific composite dish format.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of economic rice with one vegetable dish, one non-fried meat dish, and one fried meat dish, typically weighing approximately 400 g as served at a Singapore hawker centre or coffee shop.

nutrient_source.title: Economic Rice (1 Vegetable, 2 Meats (1 Fried)) — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Economic Rice (1 Vegetable, 2 Meats (1 Fried)), food code F05100034. All per-100g values are sourced from this record at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — white rice, cooked (analogous estimate for rice-dominant composite dish)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition, 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Singapore economic rice (cai fan) with this specific topping combination. GI of 72 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database value for cooked white rice (jasmine/long-grain), which is the dominant carbohydrate component of this dish. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (72 × 67.72) / 100 ≈ 48.8, using 400 g serving size and 16.93 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 272 mg/100 g (approximately 1,088 mg per 400 g serving), representing over 47% of the 2,300 mg daily limit in a single meal, with minimal potassium or dietary fibre to offset vascular load.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced sauce and soy-based condiments, substitute the fried meat with a steamed or braised option to lower both sodium and saturated fat, and limit portion size to no more than 300 g to keep single-meal sodium below 820 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI white rice base (GI 72) combined with low dietary fibre (1.03 g/100 g) produces a high glycaemic load of approximately 48.9 per standard serving, driving rapid postprandial glucose elevation; the fried meat component adds excess fat that further impairs insulin sensitivity.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should reduce the rice portion to 100–150 g, replace white rice with brown rice or mixed grain rice where available, avoid the fried meat item in favour of steamed fish or braised tofu, and pair the meal with additional non-starchy vegetables to slow glucose absorption.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat at 8.09 g/100 g with an unknown saturated fat fraction from the fried meat component, combined with 272 mg/100 g sodium and advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) generated by deep-frying, collectively elevate LDL cholesterol, arterial stiffness, and oxidative stress risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should eliminate the fried meat component and replace it with a steamed, poached, or braised lean protein, request dishes prepared with minimal oil and sauce, and limit this meal to occasional consumption given the combined sodium, fat, and AGE burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-fried meat introduces dietary AGEs that directly promote synovial inflammation and worsen RA disease activity; no anti-inflammatory nutrients (omega-3, polyphenols, antioxidants) are present in meaningful quantities.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should replace the fried meat with oily fish (e.g., grilled mackerel or sardines) or braised chicken, increase the vegetable portion, and avoid deep-fried items at every meal to reduce cumulative AGE exposure.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Economic rice, known locally in Singapore as 'cai fan' (菜饭 in Chinese), is a ubiquitous hawker-centre and coffee-shop meal consisting of steamed white rice served with a self-selected combination of pre-cooked dishes displayed in trays. This specific variant includes one vegetable dish (typically stir-fried leafy greens such as kai lan, chye sim, or cabbage), one non-fried meat dish (commonly braised pork belly, steamed fish, or stir-fried chicken), and one fried meat dish (commonly deep-fried pork chop, fried chicken wing, or ngoh hiang). The rice is the dominant caloric component, contributing the majority of the 16.93 g/100 g carbohydrate load. The fried meat component introduces additional fat from deep-frying and generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs). Per 100 g, the dish provides 174 kcal, 8.13 g protein, 8.09 g fat, 16.93 g carbohydrate, 1.03 g dietary fibre, and 272 mg sodium. The glycaemic index is high (GI 72, analogous to cooked white rice), and the glycaemic load per standard 400 g plate is approximately 48.9. Clinically, this meal is a concern for individuals managing hypertension (high sodium), type 2 diabetes (high GI, low fibre), cardiovascular disease (fat and sodium load), and rheumatoid arthritis (AGEs from frying). It is a staple working-lunch option across Singapore's multiethnic population and is sold at hawker centres, coffee shops (kopitiams), and food courts island-wide. The dish is not inherently vegetarian or vegan due to the meat components, and halal status depends on the specific stall and ingredients used.
common_user_queries: How many calories in economic rice with 1 veg and 2 meats?
Is economic rice suitable for diabetics?
How much protein in economic rice?
Can I eat cai fan if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (food code F05100034) at confidence 0.95, which is the primary approved source for Singapore hawker dishes. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using cooked white rice as the dominant carbohydrate component, clearly documented as an estimate.
notes: saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this composite hawker dish are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry F05100034 as of 2026-04-16. The HPB source provides only the core macronutrient and selected mineral values. Glycaemic load per serving calculated as GL = (72 × 16.93 × 400) / (100 × 100) = 48.9, using a 400 g standard plate serving. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on cooked white rice from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this specific composite dish combination was found in approved sources. The input dietary_tags listed 'vegetarian' and 'plant_based' which are incorrect for a dish containing meat components; these have been corrected to is_vegetarian: false and is_vegan: false. Halal status is not confirmed at the dish level as it depends on individual stall certification. Allergen profile (gluten, soy, egg) is indicative based on typical preparation methods and may vary by stall.
